paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel

caption: Fig. 1. X-ray diffraction patterns of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips.
paper_abstract: a Center for Advanced Solidification Technology (CAST), State Key Laboratory of Advanced Special Steels, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: austenite is the major matrix phase, as shown in Fig. 1. With an increase in Cr content, the ferrite phase (110) diffraction (2θ = 44.1◦) intensity increases, which means that addition of Cr promotes the formation of ferrite phase. According to XRD results by JADE, the austenite lattice constants are 3.70 Å, 3.56 Å, 3.56 Å and 3.55 Å for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips, respectively. The metallographic photos indicate that ferrite uniformly distributes in the austenite matrix, as shown in Fig. 2. According to a quantitative measurement by Gatan Digital Micrograph, the ferrite fraction is 10.2 vol %, 11.4 vol%, 14.8 vol%, and 19.6 vol% for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips, respectively. Moreover, Cr addition changes the shape of ferrite from rodlike to block like gradually. It is found from $\mathrm { F i g . } 2 \left( \mathrm { a } \right)$ that all ferrites are in the center of austenite grains, which means that δ-ferrite first nucleated and grew from melt during the near-rapid solidification. The experimental results agree with the solidification mode of this alloy: FA (ferrite-to-austenite) [2,4], i.e. L (liquid) → L+δ (high temperature ferrite) → L+δ+γ (austenite) → δ+γ.

paper_abstract: a Center for Advanced Solidification Technology (CAST), State Key Laboratory of Advanced Special Steels, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: To find the origin for the changes in mechanical properties, TEM was carried out on as-cast strips. TEM photographs of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2CxCr strips are shown in Fig. 4. It is found that there are a few dislocations in the austenite of 0Cr strip, as shown in bright-field (BF) image in Fig. 4 (a). When 3 wt% and 6 wt% Cr are added to the strips, more dislocations appear in the austenite, as shown in Fig. 4 (b, c). The dislocation density in 9Cr strip is still very high, but slightly lower than that of 6Cr strip, as shown in Fig. 4 (d). Those indicate that addition of Cr increases dislocation density in austenite matrix of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C strips. Selectedarea diffraction patterns (SADPs) are inserted in Fig. 4.

There is a clear superlattice in austenite matrix of 0Cr strip, which is characterized as κ-carbides. But the superlattice in austenite diffraction pattern of 3Cr and 6 Cr strips is very weak, as shown in Fig. 4 (b) and (c). No superlattice belonging to κ-carbides is observed in austenite diffraction pattern of 9Cr strip as shown in Fig. 4 (d). The sizes of κ-carbides in the austenite of these three strips are too small to identify, and κ-carbides are only visible at phase boundary between austenite and ferrite in dark-field (DF) image of 0Cr strip, as shown in $\mathrm { F i g . 4 ( e ) }$ . Thus, addition of Cr suppresses the precipitation of κ-carbide as the results shown in some researches [17,18]. However, some $\mathrm { C r } _ { 2 3 } \mathrm { C } _ { 6 }$ carbides are also observed at austenite grain boundary in 9Cr strip, which has a cubic structure with a lattice constant of 1.054 nm, as shown in $\mathrm { F i g . 4 ( f ) }$ . The $\mathrm { C r _ { 2 3 } C _ { 6 } }$ carbides are in granular or banded shapes, with a width of about 100 nm, which may lead to inter-granular cracking along coarsening carbides at grain boundary [23].

In this work, slip bands in austenite are observed during deformation of the strips, thus we mainly examin the slip bands of 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. Fig. 12(a) and (c) are TEM images of the microstructure in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. Average spacing between slip bands is \~90 nm and \~80 nm in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips, respectively. For Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel, average spacing between slip bands has a dependence to deformation behavior. The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually suggests higher strain hardening $[ 3 2 , 4 2 , 4 3 ]$ , which is in a good agreement with our results. The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually causes higher elongation after tensile fracture, which is not consistent with our work, because 0Cr strip with larger average spacing has higher elongation than that of 6Cr strip. From the TEM images shown in Fig. 4, there are more dislocations in austenite of as-cast 6Cr strip before stretching than that of 0Cr strip, which should be one of the reasons for low elongation of 6Cr strip. On the other hand, if a κ-carbide is sheared by a dislocation, the following dislocations can easily cut through the sheared κ-carbides rather than non-sheared κ-carbides [4,44]. As a result, slip spacing is relatively wide by the localization of the shearing due to higher obstructive of larger κ-carbides for dislocations in 0Cr strip. In the as-cast 6Cr strip, because of the smaller and less κ-carbides which are relatively easy to cut through, multi-directional shearing occur, leading to a narrow slip band spacing.

TEM images of ferrite in Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips: (a) DF image of 0Cr strip using (110)δ and corresponding SADP; (b) DF image of 3Cr strip using $( 1 \overline { { 1 0 } } ) _ { \delta }$ and corresponding SADP; (c) DF image of 6Cr strip using 20 $) " > ( 2 0 0 ) _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ and corresponding SADP; (d) DF image of 9Cr strip using $( 2 0 0 ) _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ and corresponding SADP.

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: Only a few dislocations are occasionally observed in the ferrite in all strips. In the ferrite of 0Cr and 3Cr strips, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), no superlattice is observed, and the images exhibit almost clean features without evident secondary phases. In 6Cr and 9Cr strips, a superlattice belonging to DO3 phase in ferrite is observed, as shown in Fig. 5 (c) and (d), which is determined by the fact that (hkl) are all odd or all even, and the reflections belong to DO3 phase are similar with previous results [17]. According to DF images of ferrite, nano-scale DO3 phases clearly appear in ferrite of 6Cr and 9Cr strips.

citation_sentences: The constituent phase of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr strips after annealing at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 h are still austenite and ferrite, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7. The XRD result in Fig. 6 displays that the diffraction peak (111) of κ-carbides at $4 1 . 4 ^ { \circ }$ obviously appears in annealed 0Cr strip, suggesting that annealing at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 h promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides. However, this diffraction peak is still not observed in Cr-containing annealed strips, so Cr addition also suppresses the precipitation of κ-carbides even after annealing. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the grain boundaries and dendritic structure of annealed 0Cr strip are clearly observed, while the grain boundary could not be observed in Crcontaining strips under the same conditions, indicating the addition of Cr improves corrosion resistance of steel strips as reported in the research of Tsay and Tuan [20,22,23]. Compared with as-cast strips, the strain hardening rate of all annealed strips except 9Cr strip clearly decreases. The reduction in the strain hardening rate of annealed 3Cr strip is the largest about 500 MPa.

paper_abstract: a Center for Advanced Solidification Technology (CAST), State Key Laboratory of Advanced Special Steels, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: To correlate the microstructure with the mechanical properties of annealed strips, TEM was conducted on annealed Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2CxCr strips, and the results of TEM are shown in Fig. 9 for austenite and Fig. 10 for ferrite.

After annealing at $5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 h, there are still clear superlattices of κ-carbides in austenite matrix of 0Cr strip, and its intensity increases obviously as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The superlattices of κ-carbides in SADP of annealed 3Cr and 6Cr strips are still very weak, as shown in ${ \mathrm { F i g . ~ } } 9 \left( { \mathrm { b } } , \right.$ c). No superlattice of κ-carbides in SADP is found, and only Cr C carbides with a width of about 200 nm are observed in annealed 9Cr strip, as shown in Fig. 9 (f). The dislocation density in austenite matrix of annealed Cr-containing strips is obviously reduced, compared with those in as-cast Cr-containing strips, while it is still higher than that of annealed 0Cr strip. After annealing at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 h, the superlattice belonging to DO3 phase appears in all annealed strips, including 0Cr and 3Cr, as shown in Fig. 10.

Fig. 12(b) and (d) are TEM images of annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. After tensile fracture, average spacing of 0Cr strip increases to \~190 nm, but that of 6Cr strip decreases to \~60 nm compared with as-cast strips, which is also evidenced by increase in elongation of 6Cr strip and decrease in elongation of 0Cr strip. It is an interesting phenomenon that although annealing promotes precipitation of the κ-carbides in 0Cr and 6Cr strips, which is confirmed by changing their yield strength and TEM images, an opposite change occurs in average spacing in annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips. In the annealed 0Cr strip, more κ-carbides are present with an average diameter of 14 nm precipitate in austenite, as shown in Fig. 9, and thus these coarse carbides are barriers for dislocations because dislocations can only pass through those with diameter being smaller than 10 nm [32,45], while the average diameter of κ-carbides is 8 nm in as-cast 0Cr strip.

caption: Fig. 10. TEM images of ferrite in Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr annealing at $5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 h: (a) DF image of 0Cr strip along $( 1 \overline { { 1 } } 0 ) _ { \delta }$ and corresponding SADP, (b) DF image of 3Cr strip along $( 1 \overline { { 1 0 } } ) _ { \delta }$ and corresponding SADP, (c) DF image of 6Cr strip along $( 2 0 0 ) _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ and corresponding SADP, (d) DF image of 9Cr strip along $( 2 0 0 ) _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ and corresponding SADP.

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: To confirm the reason for improvement in plasticity of annealed Crcontaining strips, the mechanical properties and microstructures of 3Cr strips were examined after different annealing time at $5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the plasticity of 3Cr strip increases gradually with an increase of annealing time, but the yield strength do not change significantly after 1 h annealing. By observing TEM images, it is found that the dislocation density gradually decreases with increasing the annealing time, as shown in Fig. 11(b), (c) and (d), respectively. The increase of yield strength is resulted from precipitation of nano-scale κ-carbides. The evolution of properties and microstructures as increasing of annealing time at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ indicates that κ-carbides are mainly precipitated in the first hour of annealing treatment, while the recovery needs longer time. So to improve the plasticity, the annealing time should be prolonged to reduce dislocation density at this annealing temperature, while longer annealing time may coarsen κ-carbides [34]. The anneal time should be further optimized to achieve a balance between the coarsening of κ-carbides and reduction of dislocation density.

citation_sentences: In this work, slip bands in austenite are observed during deformation of the strips, thus we mainly examin the slip bands of 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. Fig. 12(a) and (c) are TEM images of the microstructure in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. Average spacing between slip bands is \~90 nm and \~80 nm in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips, respectively. For Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel, average spacing between slip bands has a dependence to deformation behavior. The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually suggests higher strain hardening $[ 3 2 , 4 2 , 4 3 ]$ , which is in a good agreement with our results. The narrowed average spacing between slip bands usually causes higher elongation after tensile fracture, which is not consistent with our work, because 0Cr strip with larger average spacing has higher elongation than that of 6Cr strip. From the TEM images shown in Fig. 4, there are more dislocations in austenite of as-cast 6Cr strip before stretching than that of 0Cr strip, which should be one of the reasons for low elongation of 6Cr strip. On the other hand, if a κ-carbide is sheared by a dislocation, the following dislocations can easily cut through the sheared κ-carbides rather than non-sheared κ-carbides [4,44]. As a result, slip spacing is relatively wide by the localization of the shearing due to higher obstructive of larger κ-carbides for dislocations in 0Cr strip. In the as-cast 6Cr strip, because of the smaller and less κ-carbides which are relatively easy to cut through, multi-directional shearing occur, leading to a narrow slip band spacing.

Fig. 12(b) and (d) are TEM images of annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture. After tensile fracture, average spacing of 0Cr strip increases to \~190 nm, but that of 6Cr strip decreases to \~60 nm compared with as-cast strips, which is also evidenced by increase in elongation of 6Cr strip and decrease in elongation of 0Cr strip. It is an interesting phenomenon that although annealing promotes precipitation of the κ-carbides in 0Cr and 6Cr strips, which is confirmed by changing their yield strength and TEM images, an opposite change occurs in average spacing in annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips. In the annealed 0Cr strip, more κ-carbides are present with an average diameter of 14 nm precipitate in austenite, as shown in Fig. 9, and thus these coarse carbides are barriers for dislocations because dislocations can only pass through those with diameter being smaller than 10 nm [32,45], while the average diameter of κ-carbides is 8 nm in as-cast 0Cr strip.

A statistical measurement indicates that average widths of slip bands in as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr strips are 13.4 nm and 12.5 nm, and the average widths of slip bands in annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips are 20.5 nm and 7.9 nm, respectively, after tensile fracture as shown in Fig. 12. These indicate that average width of slip bands is inversely correlated with spacing

caption: Fig. 13. The slip band spacing and width after tensile fracture of 0Cr and 6Cr strips with different status.
paper_abstract: a Center for Advanced Solidification Technology (CAST), State Key Laboratory of Advanced Special Steels, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel
citation_sentences: between slip bands after tensile fracture. Finer slip bands and smaller average spacing after tensile fracture are usually corresponding to high ductility, and this phenomenon is termed as dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) [35,43,46]. However, although κ-carbides in as-cast 6Cr strip have the lowest content and the smallest size among three strips, the total elongation after tensile fracture is lowest (as shown in Fig. 13) due to its high dislocation density before stretching, which interferes the relationship between slip bands and ductility. It can be concluded that the dislocations play an important role in ductility of this austenitic low-density steel, besides formation ability of slip bands during deformation process.
